title: Feed-Forward Network
summary: Position-wise two-layer MLP per token. Expands to $d_{ff}$ (typically $4 d_{\text{model}}$), applies nonlinearity, compresses back. Attention = structured relational decomposition, FFN = general feature transformation.
key_insights: Modern DL = structured decomposition (attention) + general learning (FFN)
Expand-then-compress: higher dimension allows disentangling features that overlap in $d_{\text{model}}$
ReLU creates sparsity (~50% zeros), GELU is smoother, SwiGLU adds gating with 50% more params
FFN is where factual knowledge is primarily stored in LLMs
FFN is largest parameter block: $8d^2$ vs attention's $4d^2$ (for $d_{ff} = 4d$)

derivations.title: Why expansion into higher dimension helps — separability argument

steps.name: Cover's theorem (intuition)
steps.math: Points not linearly separable in $d$ dimensions become separable with high probability in $d_{ff} \gg d$ via nonlinear mapping.

steps.name: Applied to FFN
steps.math: Input $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ has entangled features. $W_1$ projects into $\mathbb{R}^{d_{ff}}$. Nonlinearity $\sigma$ creates nonlinear feature map. In $4\times$ larger space, features become separable.

steps.name: Compression as distillation
steps.math: $W_2$ projects back to $\mathbb{R}^d$ — information bottleneck selects which high-dim features matter.

derivations.consequence: Expansion ratio (typically $4\times$) = disentangling capacity. Larger = more capacity, more parameters.

derivations.title: ReLU sparsity analysis

steps.name: At initialization
steps.math: $W_1 x + b_1$ has zero-mean Gaussian entries (Xavier init). $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0,z)$ zeros negatives. For symmetric distribution: exactly 50% of $d_{ff}$ activations are zero.

steps.name: Sparsity as feature selection
steps.math: Each token $x$ activates a different subset of $d_{ff}$ neurons. This subset = token's 'active feature set'. Different tokens have different active sets.

steps.name: Effective capacity
steps.math: 50% sparsity: each token uses $\sim d_{ff}/2 = 2d$ features from pool of $4d$. Expressivity comes from combinatorial selection.

derivations.consequence: ReLU sparsity = feature selection giving each token a different 'view' of expanded space.

derivations.title: GELU vs ReLU — smoothness and gradients

steps.name: ReLU gradient
steps.math: $\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\text{ReLU}(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ 0 & z < 0 \end{cases}$. Hard threshold → dead neurons.

steps.name: GELU definition
steps.math: $\text{GELU}(x) = x \cdot \Phi(x)$ where $\Phi$ is Gaussian CDF. Approx: $\text{GELU}(x) \approx 0.5x(1 + \tanh[\sqrt{2/\pi}(x + 0.044715x^3)])$

steps.name: GELU gradient
steps.math: $\text{GELU}'(x) = \Phi(x) + x\phi(x)$. Always nonzero — no dead neurons. Smooth transition.

derivations.consequence: GELU preserves gradient flow, no dead neurons. Standard in transformers (GPT, BERT).

derivations.title: Position-wise independence
derivations.math: FFN applies same $W_1, W_2$ to each token independently: $\text{FFN}(X)[i,:] = \text{FFN}(X[i,:])$. No interaction between positions.
derivations.consequence: Token mixing = attention ONLY. FFN transforms each token in isolation. Separation: attention = routing, FFN = transformation.

geometric_properties: ReLU creates polyhedral regions — each has a different linear function
Sparsity ratio: too high ($>90\%$) = underutilized. Too low ($<20\%$) = not selective
FFN output should be in similar region as input (residual will add them)
statistical_properties: $\|\text{FFN}(x)\|$ should be comparable to $\|\text{attention output}\|$ — both added via residual
Monitor pre-activation stats: consistent saturation → gradients vanish through FFN
Dead neuron count: always-zero neurons = wasted capacity
numerical_properties: Dominant cost: $2 \times O(B n d \cdot d_{ff})$ FLOPs per block
When $n < d_{\text{model}}$: FFN dominates. When $n > d_{\text{model}}$: attention dominates ($O(n^2)$)
Memory: $\approx 8d^2$ params for $d_{ff} = 4d$. For $d = 512$: ~2.1M per block

related: TRANSFORMER.RESIDUALS — FFN output added via residual
PRODUCTION.PRUNING — FFN neurons are pruning candidates
